params.description: Scale Preview System from Warmup appears. Shows student's data with all four scale options (1, 2, 5, 10). Each scale displays a checkmark indicating it worked for that data.

beats.text: In Warmup, all four scales worked for your data. You had options!

elements.description: Scale Preview System showing student's warmup data with all four scales marked as working.

beats.text: But what happens when the numbers are bigger? Let's find out.

elements.description: Scale Preview System showing student's warmup data with all four scales marked as working.

params.description: Data table appears showing Books Read This Month. Aisha: 20, Ben: 35, Carlos: 55, Dana: 80.
Vertical bar graph appears. Books Read This Month. Axis shows 0 to 50 with scale of 5. Graph is currently empty.

beats.text: Here's data about how many books four students read this month. Dana read the most: 80 books!

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month. Aisha: 20, Ben: 35, Carlos: 55, Dana: 80.
Vertical bar graph. Books Read This Month. Axis 0 to 50, scale of 5. Empty graph.

beats.text: Let me try Scale of 5 and see what happens.

params.description: Aisha (20), Ben (35), Carlos (55) bars draw normally. Dana's bar (80) extends past the top boundary at 50. Warning indicator appears.
Aisha (20), Ben (35), Carlos (55) bars visible. Dana's bar extends past top. Warning indicator visible.

beats.text: Hmm. Carlos's bar is just above my graph, but Dana's bar goes past the top. The graph only goes up to 50 right now.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Aisha (20), Ben (35), Carlos (55) bars visible. Dana's bar extends past axis top at 50. Warning indicator visible.

beats.text: I could make the graph taller to fit 80. Let's see. I'd need 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80.

params.description: Axis extends upward. Tick marks appear sequentially: 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80. Axis now shows 0 to 80.
Axis extended to 80. All tick marks visible: 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80. Dana's bar now fits within the graph.
Axis extension complete. Dana's bar (80) now fully visible within graph boundary.

beats.text: That's a lot of numbers on the axis. 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, all the way up to 80. That's 17 tick marks.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph with axis 0 to 80, scale of 5. All 17 tick marks visible and highlighted. Aisha (20), Ben (35), Carlos (55), Dana (80) bars fully visible.

beats.text: It works, but is there a better way?
When one scale needs too many tick marks, try a bigger scale.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph with axis 0 to 80, scale of 5. All 17 tick marks visible. Aisha (20), Ben (35), Carlos (55), Dana (80) bars fully visible.

params.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month: Aisha 20, Ben 35, Carlos 55, Dana 80.
Vertical bar graph, Books Read data, scale of 5, 17 tick marks visible and highlighted (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80). All four bars visible within boundary.

beats.text: Scale of 5 works, but that's a lot of tick marks. Let's try a bigger scale.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph, Books Read data, scale of 5, 17 tick marks visible. All bars fit.

beats.text: Click Scale of 10 to see what happens.
Click Scale of 10 to see what happens.

validator.description: Student clicked Scale of 10 button, correct

params.description: Bar graph updates to scale of 10. Axis shows 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80—only 9 tick marks. All four bars fit cleanly. Checkmark indicator appears.

beats.text: That's it. Only 9 tick marks now instead of 17. Much cleaner!

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong scale button on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. The guide said to try Scale of 10.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong scale button on second attempt

params.description: Scale of 10 button highlights.

beats.text: Here's what to do: click the button that says Scale of 10. That's the one we're testing now.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong scale button three or more times. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System clicks Scale of 10 button. Graph updates to scale of 10 showing 9 tick marks.

beats.text: Let me show you. I click Scale of 10, right here. The graph changes to show tick marks at 0, 10, 20, all the way to 80. That's 9 marks total, much fewer than the 17 we had with Scale of 5. The answer is Scale of 10 because it makes the graph cleaner and easier to read.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph, Books Read data, scale of 10, 9 tick marks (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80). All bars fit cleanly. Checkmark visible.

beats.text: All the data fits, and the graph is easier to read.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph, Books Read data, scale of 10, 9 tick marks. All bars fit cleanly. Checkmark visible.

params.description: Data table appears showing Books Read This Month: Aisha 20, Ben 35, Carlos 55, Dana 80. Largest value 80 is highlighted.

beats.text: Here's the first thing to check: What's your biggest number? That tells you what scales might work.
What's the biggest number in this data?

validator.description: Student selected 80, correct

beats.text: Right. 80 is the biggest. Always check the biggest number first. Does it fit on the scale without too many lines on the axis?

validator.description: Student selected 20: confused smallest with biggest

params.description: Values 20 and 80 both highlight for comparison.

beats.text: 20 is the smallest number. We need the biggest. Which value is larger, 20 or 80?

validator.description: Student selected 35: middle value confusion

params.description: Values 35, 55, and 80 highlight to show comparison.

beats.text: 35 is one of the middle values. Look at all four numbers. Which one is greatest?

validator.description: Student selected 55: close but not the largest

params.description: Values 55 and 80 both highlight for direct comparison.

beats.text: 55 is close, but there's one value bigger. Compare 55 and 80. Which is greater?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Value 80 highlights and pulses to indicate it is the maximum.

beats.text: Let me show you. I look at each number: 20, 35, 55, 80. I compare them. 80 is greater than all the others. The biggest number is 80. That is what we check first when choosing a scale.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values: Aisha 20, Ben 35, Carlos 55, Dana 80. Value 80 highlighted.

beats.text: You saw that Scale of 5 needed 17 tick marks. Scale of 10 only needs 9. For big data, bigger scales make cleaner graphs.
When more than one scale fits, pick the one that's easier to read.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values: Aisha 20, Ben 35, Carlos 55, Dana 80.

params.description: Data table clears from screen for clean transition.

beats.text: You learned to check if the biggest number fits. But there's something else to check.

params.description: Horizontal data table appears. Marbles in Jars data. Jar A: 7, Jar B: 12, Jar C: 19, Jar D: 23.

beats.text: Here's data about marbles. Look at the biggest number: 23. That's pretty small!

elements.description: Horizontal data table showing Marbles in Jars data. Jar A: 7, Jar B: 12, Jar C: 19, Jar D: 23.

params.description: Horizontal bar graph appears with scale selector showing all four buttons. No scale selected yet. Marbles in Jars data.

beats.text: This graph is horizontal. The bars go sideways. Let's check which scales fit. Try clicking each scale.
Click each scale to see if 23 fits.

validator.description: Student clicked all four scale buttons to explore whether 23 fits on each scale. All scales fit.

beats.text: You got them all. All four scales fit! Let's look at Scale of 1.

elements.description: Horizontal data table showing Marbles in Jars data. Jar A: 7, Jar B: 12, Jar C: 19, Jar D: 23.
Horizontal bar graph with scale selector visible. All four scale options (1, 2, 5, 10) marked with checkmarks. No scale currently applied to graph. Marbles in Jars data.

beats.text: Click Scale of 1 to see what it looks like.
Click Scale of 1.

validator.description: Student clicked Scale of 1. Graph displays with scale of 1, showing 24 tick marks (0 through 23).

params.description: All 24 tick marks on the vertical axis highlight to emphasize the density.

beats.text: Right. It works. Every number lands exactly. But look at all those lines on the side!

validator.description: Student has not yet clicked all four scale buttons on first attempt

beats.text: Keep going. Try all the scale buttons to see which ones work.

validator.description: Student has not yet clicked all four scale buttons on second attempt

params.description: All four scale buttons highlight.

beats.text: Click each of the four scale buttons. We need to check if 23 fits on each one.

validator.description: Student has not clicked all four scale buttons after three attempts. System models the exploration.

params.description: System demonstrates clicking each scale button in sequence: 1, 2, 5, 10. Each displays briefly with checkmark.

beats.text: Let me show you. I click each scale button to see if 23 fits. Scale of 1 works. Scale of 2 works. Scale of 5 works. Scale of 10 works. All four scales can show 23. That's the answer.

elements.description: Horizontal data table showing Marbles in Jars data. Jar A: 7, Jar B: 12, Jar C: 19, Jar D: 23.
Horizontal bar graph set to scale of 1. Axis displays 24 tick marks (0 through 23), all highlighted. All four bars visible and fit within boundary. Marbles in Jars data.

params.description: Horizontal data table appears. Marbles in Jars. Jar A: 7, Jar B: 12, Jar C: 19, Jar D: 23.
Horizontal bar graph appears. Marbles in Jars data. Scale of 1. Axis shows 24 tick marks from 0 to 23. All bars visible.

beats.text: Scale of 1 works, but that's a lot of lines. You learned bigger scales are usually easier to read.

elements.description: Horizontal data table. Marbles in Jars. Jar A: 7, Jar B: 12, Jar C: 19, Jar D: 23.
Horizontal bar graph. Marbles in Jars data. Scale of 1. 24 tick marks visible on axis.

beats.text: Let's try Scale of 10.
Click Scale of 10 to compare.
Click Scale of 10 to compare.

validator.description: Student clicked Scale of 10 button

warning_indicators.message: 7 won't land exactly on a line or in the middle
19 won't land exactly on a line or in the middle

params.description: Graph updates to scale of 10. Axis now shows 0, 10, 20, 30. Warning indicators appear on Jar A bar (7 falls between 0 and 10) and Jar C bar (19 falls between 10 and 20).

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong scale button on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. We want to compare Scale of 10 to Scale of 1.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong scale button on second attempt

params.description: Scale of 10 button highlights.

beats.text: Let's see what happens with Scale of 10. Look for the button that shows 10.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong scale button three or more times. System demonstrates the correct action.

params.description: System clicks Scale of 10 button. Graph updates to show scale of 10 with axis marks at 0, 10, 20, 30.

beats.text: I'll click Scale of 10 for us. Now you can see the axis shows 0, 10, 20, 30. That's what we wanted to compare.

elements.description: Horizontal data table. Marbles in Jars. Jar A: 7, Jar B: 12, Jar C: 19, Jar D: 23.
Horizontal bar graph. Marbles in Jars data. Scale of 10. Axis shows 4 tick marks: 0, 10, 20, 30. Warning indicators visible on Jar A and Jar C bars.

beats.text: Hmm. Scale of 10 fits, but there are only 3 marks on the axis. Look at those warnings. Can you tell the exact value of where those bars end? Someone looking at our graph without the data table would need to guess how many marbles are shown.

params.description: Warning indicator on Jar A bar highlights. Message reads: 7 won't land exactly on a line or in the middle.

elements.description: Horizontal data table. Marbles in Jars. Jar A: 7, Jar B: 12, Jar C: 19, Jar D: 23.
Horizontal bar graph. Marbles in Jars data. Scale of 10. Jar A warning indicator highlighted, showing message: 7 won't land exactly on a line or in the middle.

beats.text: For big data, we'd pick Scale of 10 anyway. It's the easiest to read because we don't want too many lines on the axis. But these numbers are small and it's hard to find the exact location of the bar on the graph. We can do better.
When data is small, you have room to find a scale where everything lands exactly.

elements.description: Horizontal data table. Marbles in Jars. Jar A: 7, Jar B: 12, Jar C: 19, Jar D: 23.
Horizontal bar graph. Marbles in Jars data. Scale of 10. Warning indicators visible on Jar A and Jar C.

params.description: Horizontal data table appears. Marbles in Jars data. Jar A: 7, Jar B: 12, Jar C: 19, Jar D: 23.
Horizontal bar graph appears. Marbles in Jars data. Scale of 10 currently applied. Warning indicators visible on Jar A and Jar C bars.

beats.text: Let's try a smaller scale. Try a Scale of 2.
Click Scale of 2 to preview.

validator.description: Student clicked Scale of 2 button

params.description: Graph updates to Scale of 2. All bars land exactly on tick marks. Checkmark indicator appears. No warnings visible.

beats.text: Right. We can read the end of the bars clearly. All the numbers land exactly. 7 is right at 7, exactly halfway between 6 and 8. 12 is right at 12.

elements.description: Horizontal data table showing Marbles in Jars values.
Horizontal bar graph. Marbles in Jars data. Scale of 2 applied. All bars aligned exactly to tick marks. Checkmark indicator visible. No warnings.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong scale button on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Look at the ones place digits: 7, 2, 9, 3. Which scale shows any whole number exactly?

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong scale button on second attempt

params.description: Values 7, 19, 23 highlight in data table.

beats.text: Check 7, 19, and 23. They don't end in 0 or 5, so they aren't multiples of 5. Scale of 2 lets you place any whole number exactly. Which button shows Scale of 2?

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong scale button three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Graph updates to Scale of 2. System demonstrates how each bar lands exactly on a tick mark or halfway between: 7 is halfway between 6 and 8, 12 is on the line at 12, 19 is halfway between 18 and 20, 23 is halfway between 22 and 24.

beats.text: Let me show you. Scale of 2 works because half of 2 is 1. That means every whole number lands exactly on a line or halfway between two lines. Look: 7 lands halfway between 6 and 8, 12 is right on 12, 19 is halfway between 18 and 20, and 23 is halfway between 22 and 24. The correct answer is Scale of 2, the scale that shows any whole number exactly.

elements.description: Horizontal data table showing Marbles in Jars values.
Horizontal bar graph. Marbles in Jars data. Scale of 10 currently applied. Warning indicators visible. click_scale_button tool active.

beats.text: With Scale of 2, half of 2 is 1. So you can show any whole number exactly.
Scale of 2 works to show the exact value for all numbers, and for small data, the graph won't have too many marks on the axis.

elements.description: Horizontal data table showing Marbles in Jars values.
Horizontal bar graph. Marbles in Jars data. Scale of 2 applied. All bars aligned exactly. Checkmark indicator visible.

params.description: Left panel labeled 'Data Set A' displays numbers 20, 35, 55, 80 vertically with label beneath: 'Ones digits: 0 or 5'.
Right panel labeled 'Data Set B' displays numbers 7, 12, 19, 23 vertically with label beneath: 'Ones digits: 7, 2, 9, 3'.

beats.text: Here's a quick way to know if you need Scale of 2. Check the last digit of each number in the ones place.
Numbers that end in 0 or 5, like 20, 35, 55, 80, those are multiples of 5 and can be shown clearly with Scales of 5 and 10.

But look at 7, 12, 19, 23. See the digits in the ones place? 7, 2, 9, 3. None are 0 or 5. That tells you they aren't multiples of 5 so Scale of 2 might be your best choice. Check our groups. Which data set has last digits that are NOT 0 or 5?
Which data set has last digits that are NOT 0 or 5?

validator.description: Student clicked Data Set B, correct

beats.text: Right. Look at those last digits: 7, 2, 9, 3. None are 0 or 5. That's your Scale of 2 signal.

validator.description: Student clicked Data Set A on first attempt

beats.text: Check those ones place digits again. Do they include digits that aren't 0 or 5?

validator.description: Student clicked Data Set A on second attempt

params.description: Ones place digits 7, 2, 9, 3 highlight on Data Set B panel.

beats.text: Let's look at Data Set B. The ones digits are 7, 2, 9, 3. Notice none of those are 0 or 5. Which set has that pattern?

validator.description: Student clicked Data Set A three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Data Set B panel highlights. Ones place digits 7, 2, 9, 3 pulse in sequence.

beats.text: Data Set B is the answer. Look at the ones place: 7, 2, 9, 3. None of those digits are 0 or 5, so these numbers aren't multiples of 5. That's the signal that Scale of 2 will show all values exactly. When you see ones digits that aren't 0 or 5, think Scale of 2.

elements.description: Left panel labeled 'Data Set A' with numbers 20, 35, 55, 80 and label 'Ones digits: 0 or 5'.
Right panel labeled 'Data Set B' with numbers 7, 12, 19, 23 and label 'Ones digits: 7, 2, 9, 3'.

params.description: Vertical data table appears. Points Scored data. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.

beats.text: Here's data from a game. Four rounds of points: 22, 15, 8, 31.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Points Scored. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.

beats.text: Check the ones place digits: 2, 5, 8, 1. Do they all end in 0 or 5?

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Points Scored. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.

beats.text: 22, no. 15, yes. 8, no. 31, no. Three of them don't, so they aren't multiples of 5. Which scale will show all values exactly?
Which scale will show ALL values exactly?

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 2, correct

params.description: Vertical bar graph appears. Points Scored data. Scale of 2. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31. All bars fit within boundary, exact values clear.

beats.text: Right. Scale of 2. All of the bars fit well on the graph and their exact values are clear.

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 1

beats.text: Scale of 1 shows exact values, but count how many marks you'd need to reach 31.

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 5

params.description: Values 22, 8, and 31 highlight in the data table.

beats.text: Look at 22, 8, and 31. Do they end in 0 or 5? Those numbers won't land exactly on a line with Scale of 5.

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 10

params.description: All four values highlight in the data table.

beats.text: Check the ones place: 2, 5, 8, 1. Only 15 ends in 0 or 5. Scale of 10 won't show the others exactly.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: The ones place digits 2, 5, 8, 1 highlight sequentially in the data table.

beats.text: Let me show you. Check each ones place digit: 2, 5, 8, 1. Three of them aren't 0 or 5, so they aren't multiples of 5. Scale of 2 lets us show any whole number exactly because half of 2 is 1. The answer is Scale of 2.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Points Scored. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.
Vertical bar graph in reading mode. Points Scored data. Categories: Round 1, Round 2, Round 3, Round 4. Values: 22, 15, 8, 31. Scale of 2. Axis range 0 to 32.

params.description: Second vertical bar graph appears alongside first. Points Scored data. Scale of 1. Same bar heights as scale of 2 graph, only axis markings changed. Displays 32 tick marks from 0 to 31.

beats.text: Scale of 1 also shows the exact values, but it would require a lot of marks on the axis to get to the greatest value of 31. Let's consider when we might choose a scale of 1.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Points Scored. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.
Vertical bar graph in reading mode. Points Scored data. Scale of 2. Categories: Round 1, Round 2, Round 3, Round 4. Values: 22, 15, 8, 31.
Vertical bar graph in reading mode. Points Scored data. Scale of 1. Same categories and values as bar_graph_points. Axis range 0 to 31 with 32 tick marks visible.

params.description: Vertical data table appears showing Stickers Earned Today: Mia: 9, Noah: 5, Olivia: 7, Pete: 3.
Vertical bar graph with scale preview system visible. No bars showing yet. Categories Mia, Noah, Olivia, Pete on x-axis.

beats.text: Look at these numbers: 9, 5, 7, 3. They're really small.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Stickers Earned Today: Mia: 9, Noah: 5, Olivia: 7, Pete: 3.
Vertical bar graph with scale preview system. No bars displayed. Categories Mia, Noah, Olivia, Pete. Axis range 0-10.

beats.text: What happens if we try Scale of 10?
Click 'Scale of 10'

validator.description: Student clicked Scale of 10 button

params.description: Graph updates to scale of 10. Only tick marks at 0 and 10 visible. All bars appear with warning indicators.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Stickers Earned Today: Mia: 9, Noah: 5, Olivia: 7, Pete: 3.
Vertical bar graph showing Stickers Earned Today with scale of 10. Tick marks at 0 and 10. Bars visible with warning indicators on all bars.

beats.text: The bars fit, but we don't know the exact value for any of the bars.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Stickers Earned Today: Mia: 9, Noah: 5, Olivia: 7, Pete: 3.
Vertical bar graph showing Stickers Earned Today with scale of 10. Tick marks at 0 and 10. Bars visible with warning indicators.

beats.text: Think about the numbers: 9, 5, 7, 3. They don't end in 0 or 5, so we know Scale of 2 is clearer to show any number. Click Scale of 2.
Click 'Scale of 2'

validator.description: Student clicked Scale of 2 button

params.description: Graph updates to scale of 2. All bars show checkmark indicator. Clean graph with 0-10 range.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Stickers Earned Today: Mia: 9, Noah: 5, Olivia: 7, Pete: 3.
Vertical bar graph showing Stickers Earned Today with scale of 2. All bars aligned to tick marks with checkmark indicators. Axis range 0-10.

beats.text: That's better. We can see the bars end at lines or exactly halfway. Scale of 2 could work. But when numbers are this small, you have another option. Try Scale of 1. What do you notice?
Click 'Scale of 1'

validator.description: Student clicked Scale of 1 button

params.description: Graph updates to scale of 1. All bars land exactly on tick marks. Checkmark indicator visible. Clean graph with 0-10 range.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Stickers Earned Today: Mia: 9, Noah: 5, Olivia: 7, Pete: 3.
Vertical bar graph showing Stickers Earned Today with scale of 1. All bars land exactly on tick marks with checkmark indicators. Axis range 0-10.

beats.text: Every number lands right on a line. No halfway bars needed. We can read the graph accurately without having the data table.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Stickers Earned Today: Mia: 9, Noah: 5, Olivia: 7, Pete: 3.
Vertical bar graph showing Stickers Earned Today with scale of 1. All bars land exactly on tick marks with checkmark indicators. Axis range 0-10.

beats.text: For very small numbers, Scale of 1 can be a great choice. Simple and exact.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Stickers Earned Today: Mia: 9, Noah: 5, Olivia: 7, Pete: 3.
Vertical bar graph showing Stickers Earned Today with scale of 1. All bars land exactly on tick marks with checkmark indicators. Axis range 0-10.

params.description: Data table removed from screen.
Bar graph removed from screen.

beats.text: You know how to check if numbers fit, and when smaller scales are the best choice. Now you'll choose scales on your own.

params.description: Vertical data table appears showing Favorite Fruits data: Dates: 60, Cherries: 45, Bananas: 30, Apples: 15.
Scale Preview System panel appears displaying scales of 2, 5, and 10 with checkmarks indicating all three scales accommodate the Favorite Fruits dataset.

beats.text: Here's data about students' favorite fruits. The most votes went to Dates with 60. Scale of 1 doesn't make sense here because it would take 60 lines on the axis. That's too much!
Your job: pick the right scale. Which scale will you use?
Which scale will you use?

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 10, correct

beats.text: Good choice. Scale of 10 works well here.

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 2

params.description: Scale of 2 and Scale of 10 highlighted for comparison.

beats.text: Scale of 2 works, but look at how many marks that takes to reach 60. Count by 2s: that's 30 tick marks! Scale of 10 gives us the same information with only 6 marks.

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 5

params.description: Scale of 5 and Scale of 10 highlighted for comparison.

beats.text: Scale of 5 fits the data and shows exact values. But for numbers up to 60, Scale of 10 keeps the axis cleaner. Count: Scale of 5 needs 12 marks, Scale of 10 needs only 6.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Scale of 10 highlights with checkmark.

beats.text: Let me show you. The biggest number is 60. All the values end in 0 or 5, so Scale of 10 shows every value exactly. Count the marks: 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60. Only 6 marks. That's what makes it easiest to read. The answer is Scale of 10.

Why is Scale of 10 a good choice? Choose all of the reasons.

beats.text: You found some reasons. There are more. Think about the ones place digits AND how many marks each scale needs to reach 60.

params.description: Ones place digits (0, 5, 0, 5) pulse in data table.

beats.text: Some of your choices are right. But check which statements are actually true. Look at the ones digits and count how many marks each scale needs.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Scale Preview System animates comparison showing tick mark counts for each scale reaching 60.

beats.text: Here's why all four are true. First: 60, 45, 30, 15 all end in 0 or 5. That means Scale of 10 shows them precisely. Second: fewer marks means easier to read. Third: 60 fits on all three scales shown. Fourth: Scale of 2 needs 30 marks to reach 60, but Scale of 10 needs only 6. All four reasons support choosing Scale of 10.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Favorite Fruits data: Dates: 60, Cherries: 45, Bananas: 30, Apples: 15.

Which scale is best for this data?

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 2, correct

beats.text: Right. You saw that 14, 27, 33, 41 aren't multiples of 5 so we need scale of 2 to graph them precisely. Scale of 2 can work when the largest value is 41.

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 1

params.description: All four values highlight in the data table.

beats.text: Scale of 1 would need 41 tick marks to reach the biggest number. That's a lot of lines. Look at the ones place digits: 7, 4, 1, 3. Which scale shows any whole number exactly without too many marks?

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 5

params.description: All four values highlight in the data table.

beats.text: Look at the ones place: 7, 4, 1, 3. None are 0 or 5. Scale of 5 won't land exactly for these numbers. Which scale shows any whole number clearly?

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 10

params.description: All four values highlight in the data table.

beats.text: Check the ones place digits: 7, 4, 1, 3. None end in 0 or 5, so Scale of 10 won't show exact values. Which scale works for any whole number?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Ones place digits (7, 4, 1, 3) highlight in sequence.

beats.text: Let me show you. First, I check the ones place digits: 7, 4, 1, 3. None are 0 or 5, so these aren't multiples of 5. Scale of 2 works for any whole number. Half of 2 is 1, so every number lands exactly on a line or halfway between. The answer is Scale of 2.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Minutes of Practice. Guitar: 27, Piano: 14, Violin: 41, Drums: 33.

params.description: Vertical bar graph creation tool appears with Scale of 2 active. Gridlines visible at each tick mark. Minutes of Practice categories on x-axis.

beats.text: Now create the bar graph.
Set the bar heights for all 4 categories.

validator.description: Student set all 4 bar heights correctly

params.description: All four bars confirm placement with checkmarks.

validator.description: Student set bar heights incorrectly on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Look at the data table. Count up by 2s from zero to match each value.

validator.description: Student set bar heights incorrectly on second attempt

params.description: Gridlines at each tick mark highlight to show counting points.

beats.text: Here's what helps: each line on the axis goes up by 2. Find your value in the table, then count by 2s on the graph. Guitar is 27, so count 0, 2, 4, 6... up to 26, then one more to the halfway mark.

validator.description: Student set bar heights incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates placing each bar in sequence: Piano at 14, Guitar at 27 (halfway between 26 and 28), Drums at 33 (halfway between 32 and 34), Violin at 41 (halfway between 40 and 42).

beats.text: Let me walk you through this. Piano is 14, right on the line. Guitar is 27, halfway between 26 and 28. Drums is 33, halfway between 32 and 34. Violin is 41, halfway between 40 and 42. With Scale of 2, odd numbers land halfway and even numbers land exactly on lines. That's how the bar graph shows Minutes of Practice correctly.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Minutes of Practice. Guitar: 27, Piano: 14, Violin: 41, Drums: 33.
Vertical bar graph in building mode. Scale of 2. All 4 bars correctly placed: Guitar 27, Piano 14, Violin 41, Drums 33. Checkmarks visible on all bars.

beats.text: You chose the right scale and created the graph. Every bar lands exactly where it should.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Minutes of Practice. Guitar: 27, Piano: 14, Violin: 41, Drums: 33.
Vertical bar graph displaying Minutes of Practice data with all bars correctly placed at scale of 2.

params.description: Data table appears showing Team Scores. Red Team: 25, Green Team: 55, Yellow Team: 70, Blue Team: 40.

beats.text: Last one! Here are scores from four teams. Yellow Team won with 70 points.
You've done this vertical and horizontal now. Same process every time: check the numbers, choose the scale.
Which scale works best for this data?

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 10, correct choice

beats.text: Nice. All values end in 0 or 5, and the biggest is 70. Scale of 10 gives you a clean graph.

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 1

params.description: Yellow Team row highlights showing value 70.

beats.text: Scale of 1 would need 70 tick marks to reach the biggest number. That's too many lines on the axis. Think bigger.

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 2

params.description: All values highlight. Ones place digits visible: 5, 5, 0, 0.

beats.text: Look at the last digit in each number. 25, 55, 70, 40. All end in 0 or 5. That tells you a bigger scale will work.

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 5

params.description: Yellow Team row highlights showing value 70.

beats.text: Scale of 5 would work, but you'd need 14 tick marks from 0 to 70. There's a cleaner choice.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System highlights ones place digits in sequence: 5, 5, 0, 0. All end in 0 or 5.

beats.text: Let me show you. Check the ones place: 25, 55, 70, 40. All end in 0 or 5, so Scales of 5 and 10 both work. The biggest number is 70. Scale of 5 needs 14 tick marks. Scale of 10 only needs 7. Scale of 10 is the cleanest choice because it shows all values exactly with the fewest marks on the axis.

beats.text: You learned to choose scales. Check the biggest number. Does it fit without too many marks on the axis? Check the ones place digits. Are they 0 or 5 to find the exact place for the bar? For very small data, scale of 1 could work. Then pick the easiest to read.
